description: Unauthorized transfer of money from your bank account or wallet through digital means.
steps: Immediately call the National Cyber Crime Helpline at 1930 (the 'Golden Hour').
Report the transaction to your bank/wallet within 3 days for zero liability.
File a formal complaint on the 'cybercrime.gov.in' portal.
Visit the nearest Cyber Police Station with transaction screenshots.
authority: National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal & Local Cyber Cell
risk: High - Recovery success drops significantly after the first 24 hours.

description: Illegal harassment, threats, or defamation by digital loan apps or recovery agents.
steps: Record all calls and save screenshots of threatening messages/contacts.
Do not pay under threat; report the app/agent to the RBI Ombudsman (cms.rbi.org.in).
File a cybercrime complaint if they share your morphed photos or contacts.
Report the app on Google Play Store/Apple App Store for violation.
risk: High - These apps often use illegal access to your contacts to shame you.

description: Sharing private content without consent or persistent unwanted following/monitoring (physical or digital).
steps: Report the content immediately to the social media platform (Instagram/FB/X) for takedown.
File a complaint on cybercrime.gov.in under 'Women/Child Related Crime'.
For physical stalking, call 112 and file an FIR at the nearest police station.
Request a 'Content Takedown' order through the Cyber Cell.
risk: High - Stalking can escalate to physical violence if not reported early.

description: When a builder fails to hand over the keys to your home by the date promised or changes the plan without consent.
steps: Check if the project is registered under the State RERA portal.
Claim full refund with interest (MCLR + 2%) for the delay if you want to exit.
If staying, claim monthly interest for every month of delay from the builder.
File a formal complaint on the State RERA website with payment proofs.
proof_required: Possession date proof from allotment letter
risk: Moderate - RERA is very pro-buyer, but recovery of funds can take 12-18 months.

description: When a police officer refuses to record your complaint about a serious (cognizable) crime.
steps: Request a 'Zero FIR' if the crime happened elsewhere—they cannot refuse to take it and transfer it later.
Send the complaint in writing to the Superintendent of Police (SP) via Registered Post.
If still no action, file an application before a Judicial Magistrate under Section 175(3) of BNSS.
The Magistrate can then order the police to investigate.
proof_required: Postal receipt of letter to SP
authority: Superintendent of Police (SP) or Judicial Magistrate
risk: High - Requires persistent follow-up; not registering a cognizable FIR is a punishable offence for police.

description: Physical, emotional, sexual, or economic abuse within a domestic relationship.

laws.act: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005

steps: Call the Women Helpline 1091 or National Helpline 112.
Visit a Protection Officer (PO) or a Service Provider in your area.
File for a 'Protection Order' to stop the abuser from contacting or harming you.
File for 'Residence Orders' to ensure you are not thrown out of your shared household.
risk: High - Immediate safety is priority; don't wait for physical injury to report harassment.

description: The legal right to receive financial support from a spouse or child if you cannot support yourself.

laws.act: Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007

steps: File a petition for maintenance in the Family Court or Magistrate Court.
Request 'Interim Maintenance' for immediate expenses while the case is pending.
Senior citizens can approach the Maintenance Tribunal (SDM office) for a faster process.
If you have gifted property to kids and they neglect you, you can get it back under Section 23 of the Act.
proof_required: Income proof of the person you are suing
risk: Moderate - Usually successful if income is proven, but recovery can be slow if respondent hides assets.

description: Unwelcome sexual advances or hostile environment at the workplace.
steps: Check if your company has an Internal Committee (IC)—mandatory for 10+ employees.
Submit a written complaint to the IC within 3 months of the last incident.
If no IC exists, approach the Local Committee (LC) at the District Collector's office.
Keep records of all relevant emails and chats.
authority: Internal Committee (IC) or District Local Committee
risk: Medium - Retaliation is illegal; you can ask for a transfer or paid leave during the probe.

description: Failure in duty of care or unfair billing practices by hospitals/doctors.
steps: Gather all files, prescriptions, and detailed bills immediately.
For overcharging, compare with the hospital's published tariff (mandatory to display).
Lodge a complaint with the State Medical Council/National Medical Commission.
File for compensation in the Consumer Court using the 'e-Daakhil' portal.
risk: High - Requires expert medical testimony to prove negligence.

description: When a product/service is faulty or not as described by the seller.
steps: Call the National Consumer Helpline at 1800-11-4000.
Register a complaint on the 'e-Daakhil' portal (edaakhil.nic.in).
Send a formal legal notice to the company's grievance officer via email.
File a case in the District Consumer Court (no lawyer required for cases under 50L).
risk: Low - High success rate if you have the bill and evidence.

description: Right to claim compensation for road accidents or when an insurance company denies a legitimate claim.
steps: File an FIR immediately—this is the base for all insurance and MACT claims.
For insurance rejection, approach the Insurance Ombudsman (cioins.co.in) within 1 year.
For Hit & Run (if vehicle not found), the Government pays fixed compensation through the Solatium Fund.
File a MACT claim for injury or death of a family member.
risk: Moderate - Insurance cases are technical; the Ombudsman is a free alternative to courts.

description: Issues related to salary, illegal termination, or exploitative training bonds.
steps: Send a formal 'Demand Notice' via Registered Post to the HR and CEO.
Employment bonds are mostly illegal if they are purely 'in restraint of trade'—don't pay without legal advice.
File a complaint with the local Labour Commissioner.
The Labour Officer can mediate and force the company to pay arrears.
risk: Moderate - Mediation usually works; if not, Labour Court takes 1-2 years.

description: Right of a tenant to get their deposit back and not be evicted without a court order.
steps: Check your Rent Agreement; the landlord can only deduct for actual damages, not 'painting' unless mentioned.
If the landlord refuses to return the deposit, approach the Rent Authority (usually the local SDM).
They cannot cut your electricity or water to force you out—this is a punishable offence.
File a complaint with the Rent Authority for illegal eviction or harassment.
proof_required: Electricity/Water bill in your name (if applicable)
risk: Low - Rent Authorities are dedicated to fast-tracking these specific disputes.

description: Being threatened with death/injury or being physically attacked.
steps: Record the threat if it's over the phone or save the message.
Go to the nearest police station to file an FIR or a Non-Cognizable (NC) report (for minor threats).
Request a medical examination (MLC) immediately if you were physically hurt.
Ask for police protection if you feel there is an imminent threat to life.
proof_required: MLC (Medical Report) from a Govt hospital
risk: High - Threats are often precursors to physical crime; report early for prevention.

description: Inflated or incorrect bills for electricity and water services.
steps: File a written objection with the Sub-Divisional Officer (SDO) of the department.
If not solved in 30 days, approach the CGRF (Consumer Grievance Redressal Forum).
Request a 'Meter Testing' if you suspect the meter is faulty.
Apply to the Electricity Ombudsman if CGRF doesn't provide relief.
risk: Low - Most billing errors are fixed through the internal departmental process.

description: Exploitation of humans for labor or sexual acts against their will.
steps: Call the National Emergency Number 112 or Childline 1098 immediately.
Identify the nearest AHTU (Anti-Human Trafficking Unit) which exists in every district.
Victims are entitled to immediate rehabilitation and interim compensation from the Govt.
File an FIR; this is a non-bailable crime.
risk: Extremely High - High risk to life; always involve the police and NGOs (like Bachpan Bachao Andolan).

description: Rights of an individual when being arrested by police to prevent custodial torture and illegal detention.
steps: Police must inform you of the grounds of arrest and your right to bail (if bailable).
You have the right to inform one relative or friend immediately about your arrest.
A memo of arrest must be prepared and signed by at least one witness (relative or local person).
You must be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours of arrest (excluding travel time).
You have the right to consult a legal practitioner of your choice during interrogation.
risk: Critical - Illegal detention is a serious violation of fundamental rights.

description: Marrying another person while having a living spouse from a valid previous marriage.
steps: Obtain proof of the second marriage (Photos, Video, Invitation, or Witness statements).
Gather proof that the first marriage is still valid and subsisting (Marriage Certificate).
File a criminal complaint before the Magistrate for bigamy.
The first spouse can also file for a Declaration from the Family Court that the second marriage is null and void.
proof_required: Proof that first spouse was alive during second marriage
risk: High - Punishment can be up to 7 years in jail and a fine.

description: Activities that interfere with a person's right to enjoy their property or environment (noise, pollution, illegal structures).
steps: For noise after 10 PM, call 112 or your local police control room immediately.
File a written complaint with the Sub-Divisional Magistrate (SDM) for recurring nuisance.
SDM has the power to issue an order for immediate removal or cessation of the nuisance.
If it relates to an illegal structure, complain to the local Municipal Corporation.
risk: Medium - Relies on local enforcement; SDM orders are fast but require follow-up.

description: When a cheque is returned unpaid due to insufficient funds or other technical reasons.
steps: Send a Legal Notice within 30 days of receiving the return memo.
Give the issuer 15 days to pay the amount from the date of receiving the notice.
If they fail to pay, file a criminal complaint in court within 30 days after the notice period ends.
Strict adherence to these timelines is mandatory to keep the criminal case alive.
risk: High - Strict timelines; criminal liability for the issuer (up to 2 years jail).

description: Recovery of unpaid dues for services provided under a freelance or independent contract.
steps: Compile all email/WhatsApp trails proving the contract and work delivery.
Send a formal demand notice for payment via email and registered post.
If ignored, file a Summary Suit (Order 37) in Civil Court for faster recovery.
Consider MSME Samadhaan if you are registered as a Micro or Small enterprise.
risk: Moderate - Recovery is certain if proof is strong, but court processes take time.

description: Educational institutions withholding original certificates or refusing fee refunds on cancellation.
steps: No institution can legally retain your original 10th/12th/Degree certificates to force payment.
For fee refunds, cite the UGC policy which mandates 80-100% refund depending on cancellation date.
Lodge a formal grievance on the UGC e-Samadhan portal (samadhan.ugc.ac.in).
Legal notice to the institution's head can often resolve the issue without court.
risk: Low - UGC rules are absolute; institutions risk de-recognition for non-compliance.

description: Recovery for small business owners where buyers delay payment beyond 45 days.
steps: Buyer must pay within 45 days (if agreement exists) or 15 days (if no agreement).
Failure to pay leads to compound interest at 3x the RBI rate.
File a case on the MSME Samadhaan portal online.
Under Income Tax Section 43B(h), the buyer can't claim your invoice as an expense until they pay.
risk: High - Samadhaan portal is very effective for B2B recovery.

description: Compensation rights for airline passengers when flights are delayed, cancelled, or boarding is denied.

sections: CAR Section 3, Series M, Part IV

steps: For delays >2 hours, free meals and refreshments are mandatory.
For cancellations without 24h notice, airline must offer alternate flight or refund + compensation (up to ₹10k).
If denied boarding (overbooking), claim compensation up to 4x ticket price (Max ₹20k).
Lodge grievances on the AirSewa portal for fast resolution.
risk: Low - DGCA rules are strict; airlines usually comply when reported via AirSewa.

description: Guidelines on managing stray dogs and resolving conflicts in residential areas.

laws.act: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960

steps: It is illegal for RWAs or municipalities to relocate or kill stray dogs.
Dogs must be caught only for sterilization and returned to the same spot.
In case of dog bite, the municipality is liable for failure in sterilization programs; seek medical help first.
RWAs cannot stop residents from keeping pets or feeding strays in designated spots.
proof_required: Video of cruelty or illegal relocation
risk: Low - High legal protection for animals, but ground-level management varies.

description: Division of shared ancestral property among legal heirs.
steps: Obtain a Legal Heir Certificate from the Tehsildar.
Send a formal partition notice to all co-owners.
If mediation fails, file a Partition Suit in Civil Court.
Daughters have absolute equal rights in ancestral property as sons since 2005.
risk: Very High - Civil land disputes can take decades in India.

description: Unauthorized access, leak, or misuse of personal sensitive data provided to an entity.

sections: Section 43A (Compensation for failure to protect data)

steps: Report the leak to the company's Data Protection Officer (DPO) immediately.
File a complaint on the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal (cybercrime.gov.in).
For financial impact, inform your bank and block accounts if necessary.
If the entity is a major corporation, you can seek compensation under IT Act Section 43A.
proof_required: Notification from the company about the breach
authority: Cyber Police / CERT-In / Data Protection Board
risk: Medium - Proving specific damages can be technical.

description: Employment of children under 14 in any occupation or adolescents (14-18) in hazardous occupations.
steps: Call Childline 1098 immediately (24/7 toll-free).
Inform the local Police or the District Magistrate (DM).
Provide the exact location where the child is being forced to work.
Inform a local Child Rights NGO (like Bachpan Bachao Andolan).
risk: High - High urgency due to child safety risk.

description: Unauthorized taking of property belonging to another person with the intent to deprive them of it.

sections: Section 307 (Theft after preparation made for causing death, hurt or restraint)

steps: Visit the nearest police station or use the state's e-FIR portal (for unknown accused) to file an FIR immediately.
For vehicle theft, provide the Registration Number, Engine Number, and Chassis Number.
Inform your insurance company within 24-48 hours of the incident to initiate the claim process.
Inform the RTO (Regional Transport Office) about the theft to prevent illegal transfer.
If the police do not find the property within 90 days, request a 'Final Report' or 'Untraced Report' from the court for insurance settlement.
risk: High - Recovery is difficult if the FIR is delayed or the property is dismantled/sold quickly.
